column2: The 'pet vs. cattle' metaphor for servers: which property best describes 'cattle' servers?
column3: Interchangeable, recreated from a description when broken — never repaired individually
column4: Individually named and cared for; repaired when something goes wrong
column5: More expensive hardware, chosen for higher reliability
column6: Physical machines, as opposed to virtual machines

column2: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) explicitly FORBIDS one practice. Which one?
column5: Using declarative descriptions of desired state
column6: Running automated configuration tools on a schedule

column2: Ansible playbooks use which format to describe tasks?
column6: Python scripts with a special decorator

column2: The cloud-native network layer uses the Container Network Interface (CNI). What does a CNI plugin primarily do?
column3: Sets up overlay networks for distributed app containers, with isolation and resource control
column4: Manages persistent block/file storage for stateful containers
column5: Schedules containers onto cluster nodes based on resource availability
column6: Defines how container images are built, tagged, and pushed to a registry

column2: Kubernetes and Knative are both scheduling/orchestration options. For which workload type is Knative explicitly described as LESS suitable?
column5: Batch jobs with high CPU demand

column2: What distinguishes a service mesh from a single service proxy?
column3: A service mesh adds dependability, observability, and security at the PLATFORM level — without changing application code
column4: A service mesh is just multiple service proxies combined with extra application-level code
column5: A service mesh only handles TLS termination; proxies handle load balancing
column6: A service mesh replaces the service registry, making etcd unnecessary

column2: In Continuous Integration (CI), what happens AUTOMATICALLY when a developer pushes code to the repository?
column3: A new artifact is built and the test suite is run
column4: The code is immediately deployed to all production instances
column5: All running instances are restarted to pick up the new code
column6: A human reviewer must approve the change before anything runs

column2: In GitOps, what happens if someone manually modifies the running Kubernetes cluster (outside of Git)?
column3: The change is automatically rolled back to match the Git repository
column4: The Git repository is updated to match the cluster's new state
column5: An alert is raised but no automated action is taken
column6: The GitOps agent pauses synchronisation until a human resolves the conflict

column2: What are the THREE pillars of observability as described in the CNCF landscape?
column5: CPU utilization, memory usage, and network throughput

column2: In Kubernetes, what does an Operator do?
column3: Implements a reconciliation loop that brings actual cluster state towards the desired state declared in a Custom Resource
column4: Provides a web UI for managing Kubernetes deployments
column5: Replaces etcd as the backing distributed key/value store
column6: Automatically scales pods based solely on CPU utilisation
